text: I would not compare myself to Kay or Victor, but I'm inspired by their work, and I am working on a creative coding tool for graphics and 3D modeling. I've used it to create 3D printed models and simple 2D and art pieces. I am specifically inspired by the idea that code is a form of artistic expression, and that parametric/algorithmic art is an expressive artistic medium. (<http://github.com/curv3d/curv|github.com/curv3d/curv> for more details)

text:
The demoscene might count.

Also, Jennifer Jacobs and her cohort at MIT: <http://jenniferjacobs.mat.ucsb.edu>

(Spoiler: Jennifer is the guest on the next episode of the podcast, which should be out in about a week)

Not in the same league as the above, but my FoC project Hest is primarily a drawing tool that _just so happens_ to be a general purpose programming language, with an explicit goal of making programming _look cool_.

text: Your coverage graph shows some nodes with horizontal stripes, some with vertical stripes, and some with both. Does that have any significance?

elements.text: Your coverage graph shows some nodes with horizontal stripes, some with vertical stripes, and some with both. Does that have any significance?

text: <@UCUSW7WVD> In Beads, a program consists of a declaration phase, and then a series of code chunks. Each chunk is prefixed by its purpose. There are drawing blocks of various kinds, and then plain computational blocks (which are just simple functions in other languages).  There are horizontal slice/scroll chunks, vertical slice/scroll chunks, grid chunks, and freeform drawing chunks each of which is a green box, and then computational blocks are represented by circles. The icons are a hint as to the purpose of that chunk of code. Drawing blocks carry a graphical context, an implied DPI and variables.
